bins.name: Study deposits for data center loads
bins.desc: Tesla supports applying study deposit, readiness, and withdrawal penalty requirements, but specifically to large loads presumed to be data centers or crypto mining operations. This targeted approach addresses duplicative interconnection requests from this category while potentially enabling credit back through transmission credits for other applicants.
quotes: we believe that requirements should be instituted for large loads presumed to be data centers or crypto mining centers

bins.name: Hybrid facility colocation incentives
bins.desc: Tesla strongly endorses studying hybrid facilities based on the amount of injection and withdrawal rights requested, interpreting this as an incentive to drive colocation and efficiency buildout. This principle aligns with utility service responsibility based on withdrawal rights.
quotes: hybrid facilities should be studied based on the amount of injection and/or withdrawal rights requested to incentivize colocation and efficiency buildout

bins.name: Behind-the-meter capacity and POI limits
bins.desc: Tesla proposes that behind-the-meter BESS and load additions at large load facilities count toward POI limits and that facilities remain responsible for ensuring total demand stays below contracted capacity. Tesla also advocates for expedited review of capacity additions offsetting generation loss at the same POI, provided system import/export limitations remain consistent.
quotes: any additional load or battery energy storage systems (BESS) installed behind-the-meter (BTM) at large load facilities count towards the point of interconnection (POI) limit

capacity additions behind the same POI that offset generation loss to serve load should be permitted with expedited reviews, assuming the system maintains consistent or similar POI import/export limitations

bins.name: Standardized reliability standards and streamlined requirements
bins.desc: Tesla recommends establishing minimum interconnection standards applied uniformly across regions (ramp rate, load stability, voltage ride-through) using a technology-neutral lens. Once a load meets these standards, additional information about its makeup should not be required, particularly at early development stages when such details may be unavailable. This addresses the need for greater clarity, efficiency, and predictability as large load growth accelerates.
quotes: Tesla recommends establishing minimum interconnection standards for large loads, including ramp rate, load stability, and voltage ride-through rate as standardized with the process. These should be applied at the POI and with a technology-neutral lens.

if a load meets the minimum interconnection standards, additional information on its makeup, which may change over time, should not be required--especially at early stages in the load's development when it may not be available

This experience highlights a broader need to establish greater clarity, efficiency, and predictability in the interconnection process as large load growth driven by manufacturing facilities and data centers continues at a rapid pace.

bins.name: Regional best practices from SPP and ERCOT
bins.desc: Tesla notes that similar performance expectations for interconnection standards are being applied in regions like SPP and ERCOT, citing these as potential foundations for national best practices.
quotes: In 2024, Tesla pursued interconnection for the Gigafactory Texas Megapack project, declaring the use case as back-up generation to load. The interconnection process bucket was unclear for this application and, therefore, ERCOT developed a pathway.

bins.name: Clarity on BESS registration and classification
bins.desc: Tesla's experience at Gigafactory Texas revealed that the interconnection process bucket was unclear for BESS as backup generation, requiring ERCOT to develop a novel pathway. Tesla later discovered that non-exporting BESS backup generators do not require registration, a clarification validated by ERCOT's Large Load Working Group. This highlights the need for standardized, predictable interconnection pathways for BESS.
quotes: In 2024, Tesla pursued interconnection for the Gigafactory Texas Megapack project, declaring the use case as back-up generation to load. The interconnection process bucket was unclear for this application and, therefore, ERCOT developed a pathway.

Tesla reevaluated regulatory requirements and discovered that a non-exporting BESS back-up generator did not require ERCOT registration. This finding was initially validated by ERCOT's presentation to the Texas House of Representatives Committee on State Affairs and later reinforced by ERCOT's Large Load Working Group

bins.name: Distributed energy resources and virtual power plants
bins.desc: Tesla urges the Commission to recognize DERs and VPPs as meaningful grid service tools capable of supporting faster large load interconnection by alleviating transmission network constraints and capacity needs. Tesla emphasizes its leadership role operating over 1 million distributed batteries and 250,000 battery units participating in grid services, and notes VPPs can be deployed in weeks to months versus the years-long construction timelines of conventional generation.
quotes: Tesla urges the recognition of DERs and VPPs as meaningful grid service tools that can support faster interconnection of large loads to the transmission system. VPPs should be considered for their potential to alleviate transmission network constraints and capacity needs associated with the interconnection of large loads.

bins.name: Order 2222 implementation barriers
bins.desc: Tesla identifies restrictive interpretations of Order 2222 as limiting DER and VPP participation in wholesale electricity markets. Key barriers include metering and telemetry constraints, device size thresholds, state-level restrictions, and the lack of pathways for DERs receiving retail rate credits. Tesla believes these barriers can be addressed while preventing double-counting.
quotes: FERC must address challenges in 2222 implementations including: 1) metering and telemetry barriers, 2) device size thresholds, 3) state-level barriers, and particularly 4) the lack of pathways for DERs receiving retail rate credits
